description: This chart plots mass density (left y-axis, g/cm³) and density reduction percentage (right y-axis, %) as a function of aluminium content (x-axis, wt.%) for Fe-Mn-Al-C steels. A black square marks the conventional steel regime at 7.874 g/cm³. A blue circle represents conventional TRIP/TWIP steels at approximately 3 wt.% Al and ~7.30 g/cm³. Green squares denote low-density variants categorized by manganese content: <18 % Mn, 18–26 % Mn, and 26–30 % Mn. As aluminium content increases from ~6 wt.% to ~13 wt.%, mass density decreases monotonically from ~7.15 to ~6.45 g/cm³, corresponding to density reductions increasing from ~8 % to ~18 %. The data demonstrate that aluminium addition is a powerful alloying strategy to reduce steel density while maintaining the TWIP effect, supporting the development of low-density Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic steels with up to 18 % density reduction compared to conventional steels.

Figure 2 presents a scatter plot comparing the specific energy absorption (E_spec, in J/mm³) of selected Fe-Mn-Al-C steels against conventional deep-drawing steels as a function of aluminium content (wt.%). The x-axis spans approximately 5.5 to 11.5 wt.% Al, while the y-axis covers 0 to 0.6 J/mm³. Conventional steels—including high strength IF steel, HC300LA–ZStE300, and DC04–FeP04—cluster at lower Al contents (~5.5–6 wt.%) with E_spec values around 0.15–0.20 J/mm³. In contrast, Fe-Mn-Al-C alloys, tested at deformation rates of 10⁻⁴ s⁻¹ and 10⁻³ s⁻¹ according to the legend, exhibit markedly higher specific energy absorption ranging from roughly 0.25 to 0.55 J/mm³ across Al contents of ~6.5 to 11.5 wt.%. The data demonstrate that Fe-Mn-Al-C alloys possess substantially enhanced energy absorption capacity relative to traditional deep-drawing grades. This figure supports the paper’s central claim that austenitic Fe-Mn-Al-C steels offer superior strain hardening and high energy absorption together with significant density reduction, making them attractive lightweight structural materials.

Figure 3 presents a four-panel overview of mechanical properties from Rapid Alloy Prototyping (RAP) experiments on Fe-30Mn-1.2C-xAl austenitic steels (x = 0, 2, 4, 6, 8 wt.% Al) as a function of aluminum content and aging treatment. Subplots (a), (b), (c), and (d) plot yield stress (YS, MPa), ultimate tensile stress (UTS, MPa), total elongation (TE, %), and hardness (HBW), respectively. Data series include the as-homogenised condition and various aging treatments: 450°C/1h, 500°C/1h, 550°C/1h, 600°C/1h, 500°C/24h, 550°C/24h, and 600°C/24h. For Al contents up to 6 wt.%, all properties remain relatively stable across treatments. At 8 wt.% Al, a pronounced divergence occurs: prolonged aging at 550–600°C (particularly 550°C/24h and 600°C/24h) causes sharp increases in YS (~800–950 MPa), UTS (~850–950 MPa), and hardness (~320–360 HBW), but precipitously drops TE to ~10%. Conversely, the as-homogenised and short-term/low-temperature aged 8Al alloys retain high ductility (~60–80% TE) with modest strength. This trade-off confirms that kappa-carbide precipitation during intermediate-temperature aging of high-Al compositions provides substantial age-hardening at the cost of tensile ductility.

The figure is a scatter plot mapping tensile strength against total elongation to fracture for a combinatorial series of Fe-30Mn-1.2C-xAl low-density austenitic steels, with x = 0, 2, 4, 6, and 8 wt.% Al (labeled 0Al, 2Al, 4Al, 6Al, and 8Al). Data points are color-coded by Al content and correspond to tempering/aging treatments at 450, 500, 550, and 600 °C for 1 h (T) and 24 h (T+t). The 0Al alloy traces along a black dashed curve from roughly 500 MPa and 10 % elongation toward approximately 800 MPa and 77 % elongation, reflecting its superior ductility in the homogenized condition. In contrast, the 8Al alloy follows a purple dashed trajectory under T+t conditions, achieving high tensile strengths near 880–960 MPa at moderate elongations of about 25–35 %. Alloys with intermediate Al contents (2Al–6Al) populate the central region and exhibit comparatively weak sensitivity to aging time and temperature. The caption and accompanying text attribute the pronounced age strengthening in the 8Al alloy to the formation of nanosized κ-carbides, whereas the Al-free alloy suffers embrittlement during prolonged aging. This figure thus captures the distinct microstructure–property relations across the investigated Fe-Mn-Al-C alloy design space.

Figure 5 presents a correlative atom probe tomography (APT) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) investigation of nanosized κ-carbide precipitates in a Fe-30Mn-1.2C-8Al (wt.%) low-density austenitic steel synthesized by the RAP process and aged at 600°C for 24 h. Subfigure (a) shows an APT three-dimensional reconstruction where κ-carbides (L'1₂ structure) are rendered as purple iso-surfaces at 9 at.% C, embedded in a point cloud of C, Mn, and Al atoms with crystallographic directions [001] and [100] indicated. Below the reconstruction, a one-dimensional compositional depth profile extracted along a cylindrical region traversing multiple precipitates displays concentration oscillations of Fe, Mn, Al, and C (at.% versus distance in nm), revealing C and Al enrichment and Fe depletion within the κ-carbide zones. Subfigure (b) is a TEM micrograph (scale bar 20 nm) showing the regularly arranged nanosized cuboidal or rod-like κ-carbides within the austenitic matrix. Together, these data confirm the nanoscale dimensions, periodic distribution, and approximate (Fe,Mn)₃AlC stoichiometry of the κ-carbides. As stated in the caption and text, these precipitates are thermally very stable at 600°C, persisting after 100 h with only modest coarsening, and the associated deformation mechanism involves Orowan bypassing of longitudinal carbide rods.

This figure presents bulk combinatorial screening results for Fe–20Mn–0.4C–xAl (wt.%) low-density TWIP steels. The upper four panels show mechanical property data plotted against Al content (0–11 wt.%): yield strength, ultimate tensile strength (UTS), tensile elongation, and hardness, each compared across as-quenched and tempered states (400°C, 500°C, and 600°C for 24 h). Yield strength and UTS generally increase with Al content—rising markedly above 8 wt.% Al—while tensile elongation peaks near 2 wt.% Al at roughly 80–85 % and then declines sharply at higher Al levels; hardness follows a similar trend to strength. The lower four panels display corresponding XRD-derived phase-fraction bar charts for 0Al, 2Al, 8Al, and 11Al alloys, showing the volume fractions of γ austenite, α ferrite, ε martensite, and κ carbide as a function of tempering temperature. The 2Al alloy remains nearly fully austenitic, whereas 8Al and 11Al exhibit substantial α ferrite fractions, and κ carbide appears in the 11Al alloy at 600°C. Collectively, the data link the observed strengthening and embrittlement primarily to increasing ferrite content rather than to κ carbide or ε martensite formation.

The figure displays true stress–true strain compression curves for a Fe-22Mn-0.6C twinning-induced plasticity (TWIP) steel tested at seven temperatures spanning 293 K to 873 K. Each temperature condition is represented by a pair of curves: a solid line indicating the simulation prediction and star-shaped markers denoting the experimental measurement. The data show that the flow stress systematically decreases as temperature increases. At lower temperatures (293 K–573 K), the stress rises monotonically with strain, reflecting strong work hardening. At intermediate temperatures (673 K), hardening persists but at a reduced rate. At high temperatures (773 K and 873 K), the curves exhibit a distinct stress peak followed by thermal softening. Overall, the simulations capture the temperature-dependent hardening and softening behavior across the full range using a single coherent parameter set, supporting the validation of the crystal plasticity constitutive model for this low-density Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic steel system.

This figure presents the strain-hardening curves (hardening rate versus true strain) of a Fe-22wt.%Mn-0.6wt.%C model TWIP steel under compression across seven temperatures from 293 K to 873 K. Each temperature series contains experimental data (cross markers) and corresponding simulation results (solid lines), using one coherent parameter set for the entire temperature range. The y-axis reports hardening in GPa and the x-axis shows true strain up to 0.5. At lower temperatures (293 K–573 K), the curves display higher initial hardening rates near 3.5 GPa and complex non-monotonic evolution, while elevated temperatures progressively suppress hardening magnitude, producing lower and more gradually declining curves at 773 K and 873 K. The close match between experimental scatter points and simulated curves across all temperatures demonstrates the robustness of the fcc strain-hardening model for low-stacking-fault-energy alloys, supporting the conclusion that a unified constitutive framework can accurately capture temperature-dependent twinning and dislocation hardening without temperature-specific parameter adjustment.

This figure presents a correlative multi-scale microscopy investigation of hydrogen-assisted intergranular damage in a hydrogen-charged low-density Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic steel. Panel (a) is an EBSD inverse-pole-figure (RD-IPF) map showing grain orientations surrounding a crack path (black regions), with the reference direction parallel to the longitudinal/tensile axis (RD/TA). Panel (b) provides the corresponding kernel average misorientation (KAM) map derived from EBSD orientation gradients using a first-neighbor shell at a 300 nm step size; the color scale spans 0 to 5, where elevated values (green to red) localize along the crack and neighboring grain boundaries, indicating concentrated plastic strain and lattice rotation. Panel (c) is an orientation-optimized ECCI micrograph of the region bounded by white dashed lines in (b), acquired at 5.10 kX and 15.00 kV. It directly resolves the operative micromechanism: pronounced planar slip localization bands impinging on a grain boundary (dashed line) between grain A and grain B, accompanied by the nucleation and linkage of microvoids (arrows) along that boundary. The correlation demonstrates that hydrogen embrittlement in this steel involves intense slip localization—linked in the accompanying text to the presence of κ-carbides—which drives void coalescence and intergranular cracking. Scale bars are 50 μm for (a) and (b), and 10 μm for (c).

Figure 9 presents the hydrogen embrittlement response of a low-density Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic steel, specifically the kappa-carbide-strengthened Fe-26Mn-11Al-1.2C grade. The main panel shows engineering stress versus engineering plastic strain curves for specimens tested without hydrogen charging (black curves) and with hydrogen charging (red curves) under a protocol of 40 min precharging plus continuous in situ charging during tensile testing. The uncharged material sustains stresses near 1000 MPa and exhibits large ductility exceeding 40% plastic strain, whereas the hydrogen-charged specimens fracture prematurely at only ~2–5% plastic strain, revealing severe hydrogen-induced loss of ductility. The inset displays a thermal desorption analysis spectrum measured at a heating rate of 26 K/min, showing hydrogen desorption rate (wt ppm/s) versus temperature with the experimental curve, peak fit, and cumulative fit. The spectrum exhibits multiple desorption peaks; as the caption reports, these are associated with hydrogen trapping at kappa-carbide precipitates, with an estimated activation energy of ~76–80 kJ/mol from Redlich-Kister analysis. Together, the panels establish that kappa-carbides serve as hydrogen trap sites and that their presence markedly increases susceptibility to hydrogen embrittlement.
